misconceptionSummary: Students multiply all digits by the multiplier but forget to account for place value when recombining.

steps.narration: The student correctly multiplied each digit but wrote them in the wrong order — nine hundred and thirty-six instead of nine hundred and thirty-six. Wait — that happens to be the same. Let me use a clearer example.

lines.text: Multiplied left-to-right, wrote left-to-right → right order but wrong column

steps.narration: For two hundred and thirteen times three: the correct answer is six hundred and thirty-nine. But students who multiply left to right and write in sequence will get the digits in the right order only by accident — they must understand which column each product belongs in.
Multiply right to left: ones digit first, then tens, then hundreds. Each product is placed in its own column. This guarantees the correct place value for every digit.

visuals.label: Multiply right to left — each product goes in its own column

steps.narration: Six hundred and thirty-nine. Always multiply from the ones column rightwards — that ensures every product lands in the correct place.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: multiplying digits left to right and writing the results as if they are independent digits. Always work right to left — ones, then tens, then hundreds — placing each product in its column.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Work right to left and place each product in its correct column. The ones digit's product goes in the ones column — not wherever it happens to land in a left-to-right sequence.
